parts.prompt:
Chlorine gas, Cl
(g), is initially present in the container at a pressure of 0.40 atm.
(i) How many moles of Cl
(g) are in the container?
(ii) How many grams of carbon disulfide, CS
(g), are needed to react completely with the Cl
(g) ?

alternatives.correct_example: Chlorine gas, Cl,(g), is initially present in the container at a pressure of 0.40 atm.

parts.prompt:
At 30°C the reaction is thermodynamically favorable, but no reaction is observed to occur. However, at
120°C, the reaction occurs at an observable rate.
(i) Explain how the higher temperature affects the collisions between the reactant molecules so that the
reaction occurs at an observable rate at 120°C.
(ii) The graph below shows a distribution for the collision energies of reactant molecules at 120°C. Draw
a second curve on the graph that shows the distribution for the collision energies of reactant molecules
at 30°C.

S Cl is a product of the reaction.
(i) In the box below, complete the Lewis electron-dot diagram for the S
Cl
molecule by drawing in all of
the electron pairs.
Cl S S Cl
(ii) What is the approximate value of the Cl−S−S bond angle in the S
Cl
molecule that you drew in
part (c)(i) ? (If the two Cl−S−S bond angles are not equal, include both angles.)

CCl
(g) can also be produced by reacting CHCl
(g) with Cl
(g) at 400°C, as represented by the equation
below.
CHCl (g) + Cl (g) → CCl (g) + HCl(g)
3 2 4
At the completion of the reaction a chemist successfully separates the CCl
(g) from the HCl(g) by cooling
the mixture to 70°C, at which temperature the CCl
(g) condenses while the HCl(g) remains in the gaseous
state.
(i) Identify all types of intermolecular forces present in HCl(l).
(ii) What can be inferred about the relative strengths of the intermolecular forces in CCl (l) and HCl(l) ?
Justify your answer in terms of the information above.

alternatives.correct_example: CC14(g) can also be produced by reacting CHC13(g) with Cl2(g) at 400°C, as represented by the equation below. CCl4(g) + HCJ(g) At the completion of the reaction a chemist successfully separates the C
